name: Flat-Adaptive Inverse-Square Normal Form with Kelvin-Mellin Structure
description: Within the Flat Adaptive \pi_f framework, the power-law branch \pi_f(r)=\pi\lambda_0(r/r_0)^\beta induces the radial operator \Delta_f^{rad}u=u''+((\beta+1)/r)u'. Under the half-density substitution \psi=\sqrt{w_f}\,u, this operator is exactly conjugate to the one-dimensional inverse-square Schr\u00f6dinger operator -\partial_r^2+(\beta^2-1)/(4r^2). The same exponent \beta also controls the effective-dimension identity d_eff=\beta+2, Kelvin covariance under r\mapsto1/r, and the Mellin symbol m_\beta(s)=s(s+\beta) with reflection law s\mapsto-\beta-s. The novelty claim is not that inverse-square potentials or Hardy inequalities are new in themselves, but that the proposed flat-adaptive radial branch organizes these exact structures under one constitutive exponent inside a framework that explicitly remains separate from ordinary Euclidean \pi.
assumptions: Power-law flat branch: pi_f(r)=pi lambda_0 (r/r_0)^beta with lambda_0>0
Shell weight w_f(r)=2 pi_f(r) r is positive and smooth on the domain
For the weighted Hardy statement, u belongs to C_c^infty(0,infty)
evidence: Exact derivation from the shell weight and flat-adaptive radial operator
Exact Mellin symbol: m_beta(s)=s(s+beta) with reflection s->-beta-s
Sharp weighted Hardy lower bound with constant beta^2/4
Explicit annular conductance and capacity threshold at beta=0
novelty: The novelty is the exact assembly of effective dimension, inverse-square normal form, Kelvin symmetry, Mellin reflection, and conductance/capacity consequences inside one flat-adaptive radial branch controlled by beta.
limits: Not a replacement for Euclidean pi
Not claimed as established physical law
Most natural in the power-law branch
